articles.headline: England's Dan Burn relishing opportunity to battle with Norway's Erling Haaland
articles.description: Dan Burn has said he believes England must be far better than they were against Mexico to beat Norway and would relish the challenge of keeping Erling Haaland quiet.

images.credit: Photo by Cesar Gomez/Jam Media/Getty Images

article.headline: 10-man England win thriller against Mexico to reach World Cup quarterfinals
article.description: Jude Bellingham was the star with a first-half brace, while Harry Kane's penalty would prove the difference maker.
article.linkText: 10-man England win thriller against Mexico to reach World Cup quarterfinals

video.headline: England's Burn: I'll never forget the win over Mexico
video.shortHeadline: England's Burn: I'll never forget the win over Mexico
video.caption: England's Burn: I'll never forget the win over Mexico
video.title: England's Burn: I'll never forget the win over Mexico
video.description: England's Burn: I'll never forget the win over Mexico
video.shortDescription: England's Burn: I'll never forget the win over Mexico

tracking.trackingName: COM_SOC Presser (England’s Burn: I’ll never forget the win over Mexico) 2027/07/06

article.story:
England overcame hostility, altitude and Jarell Quansah's red card as Thomas Tuchel's side edged a 3-2 blockbuster against World Cup co-hosts Mexico to set-up a quarter-final clash with Norway.

The back-to-back European Championship finalists faced all manner of obstacles heading into Sunday's last-16 tie at the majestic Estadio Azteca, where kick-off was delayed by an hour due to a storm.

England started well 2,200 metres above sea level when the last-16 tie eventually got under way, with Jude Bellingham's quickfire brace silencing supporters whose hopes would be revived by Julian Quinones before the break.

Mexican belief grew further when right-back Quansah was sent off for a clumsy challenge early in the second half, only for Tuchel's team to immediately respond through skipper Harry Kane's spot-kick.

Raul Jimenez added a penalty of his own to increase English nerves, yet the visitors showed defensive nous and togetherness to win on a wild night in Mexico City. A date with Norway in Miami is the reward.

'Football's Coming Home' rung around the famous stadium at the end of a match that began with a start as intense as the atmosphere. Early on, England effectively took the sting out of proceedings, although Jordan Pickford had to produce a world-class stop to deny Jimenez's header.

The visitors got through to the hydration break unscathed and soon began to pose problems at the other end.

Anthony Gordon forced a save from Raul Rangel and an outstanding break brought a 36th-minute opener in Mexico City.  Pickford plucked out a pass and threw out to Declan Rice, who drove from deep in his own half and played wide to Bukayo Saka. The winger sent a fine clipped cross that Bellingham headed home.

Inside two minutes, England doubled their lead.  Pressure from Elliot Anderson and Gordon saw England gain possession, with Bellingham playing wide to Kane and meeting the skipper's low ball from close range.

That moment took the electricity out of the Azteca, but El Tri's fans were not silenced for long. Items cascaded down the stands as Mexico celebrated reducing the deficit as Quinones lasered home after England failed to deal with a free kick.

Tuchel's side had to hold on for dear life after that. Jimenez struck wide and then saw a header tipped over by Pickford, who roared in delight after Bellingham's vital challenge on Cesar Montes. England looked rejuvenated by the break and Nico O'Reilly saw an effort deflect onto a post, but they soon faced another barrier to progress.

The benches clashed as Quansah's challenge on Jesus Gallardo initially went unpunished, only for referee Alireza Faghani to be sent to the pitchside monitor and return brandishing a red card.

John Stones replaced Saka as Tuchel bolstered his backline after a 54th minute sending off that his side responded well to, with England restoring their two-goal advantage six minutes later.

Faghani pointed to the spot after Gordon was brought down by Rangel with Kane showing nerves of steel to strike the resulting penalty beyond the goalkeeper. But Mexico, roared on by incredible support, did not give in.

VAR Nicolas Gallo sent referee Faghani to the monitor for a penalty review and adjudged Kane to have caught Brian Gutierrez when clearing. Jimenez beat Pickford with 69 minutes on the clock and set up a thrilling finish. 

Tuchel made defence-minded changes during the second-half break as England battened down the hatches. Key interventions (especially from substitute Dan Burn) and strong goalkeeping helped keep increasingly desperate Mexico at bay, with penalty appeals waved away.

Stones prodded just wide of his own goal after 11 minutes of stoppage time had elapsed, with England players pouring onto the pitch at full-time.

keyEvents.text: First Half begins.
Declan Rice (England) is shown the yellow card for a bad foul.

Delay over. They are ready to continue.
Delay in match for a drinks break.
Delay over. They are ready to continue.
Goal! Mexico 0, England 1. Jude Bellingham (England) header from very close range to the bottom left corner. Assisted by Bukayo Saka with a cross.
Goal! Mexico 0, England 2. Jude Bellingham (England) right footed shot from very close range to the bottom left corner. Assisted by Harry Kane.
Goal! Mexico 1, England 2. Julián Quiñones (Mexico) right footed shot from the centre of the box to the high centre of the goal following a set piece situation.
Substitution, Mexico. Edson Álvarez replaces César Montes.
Jarell Quansah (England) is shown the red card.
Substitution, England. John Stones replaces Bukayo Saka.
Goal! Mexico 1, England 3. Harry Kane (England) converts the penalty with a right footed shot to the bottom left corner.
Substitution, Mexico. Santiago Giménez replaces Gilberto Mora.
Substitution, Mexico. Brian Gutiérrez replaces Luis Romo.
Marc Guéhi (England) is shown the yellow card.
Goal! Mexico 2, England 3. Raúl Jiménez (Mexico) converts the penalty with a right footed shot to the bottom left corner.
Delay in match because of an injury Nico O'Reilly (England).
Jorge Sánchez (Mexico) is shown the yellow card.
Delay over. They are ready to continue.
Delay in match for a drinks break.
Nico O'Reilly (England) is shown the yellow card.
Delay over. They are ready to continue.
Substitution, England. Djed Spence replaces Nico O'Reilly.
Substitution, England. Dan Burn replaces Elliot Anderson.
Substitution, Mexico. Álvaro Fidalgo replaces Jorge Sánchez.
Substitution, Mexico. Guillermo Martínez replaces Julián Quiñones.
Substitution, England. Morgan Rogers replaces Harry Kane.
Johan Vásquez (Mexico) is shown the yellow card.
Jordan Henderson (England) is shown the yellow card.
Second Half ends, Mexico 2, England 3.

stats.name: aerialsLost
stats.description: Total number of duels where the ball is in the air and possession is lost

stats.name: aerialsWon
stats.description: Total number of duels where the ball is in the air and possession is won

stats.name: duelsLost
stats.description: Total number of duels over the possession of the ball where a player loses the ball - doesn't include 'overrun' situations where the attacking player takes on an opponent but the ball runs away from them out of play or to an opponent.

stats.name: duelsWon
stats.description: Total number of duels for possession of the ball where possession was won (aerial won + ground duels won)

stats.name: groundDuelsWon
stats.description: Total number of duels where possession is won and ball is on the ground; including total contests, total tackles, fouls, being fouled, dispossessions and lost challenges.

stats.name: duels
stats.description: Total number of direct player-on-player confrontations for possession of the ball (aerial + ground duels)

stats.name: groundDuels
stats.description: Total number of duels over possession where ball is on the ground; including total contests, total tackles, fouls, being fouled, dispossessions and lost challenges - doesn't include 'overrun' situations where the attacking player takes on an opponent but the ball runs away from them out of play or to an opponent.
